prompt: Here's the blueprint for an admin announcement banner (announcement-banner-blueprint.md in this folder). Design the system for it — it's a fairly small feature, standard Node/Express + Postgres app with a React frontend. Produce the design doc.
assertions: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.

The design stores per-user dismissal server-side so it persists across a user's devices and sessions — it does NOT rely on client-only storage (localStorage or cookies), which would fail the blueprint's cross-device requirement. (Correctness trap: client-side dismissal is the easy wrong answer.)

The design is proportional to a small feature: it does not introduce infrastructure the feature does not need (no message queue, websockets, Redis, or a background job purely to expire banners — expiry is handled at query time), and any such omission is a deliberate, stated choice.

The 'one active banner per workspace' rule is enforced structurally (e.g. a partial unique index) so that two admins publishing concurrently resolve deterministically rather than leaving two active banners.
Dismissal is keyed to the banner's identity, not its text, so that an admin editing the banner text does not resurface it for users who already dismissed it — matching the blueprint rule explicitly.
Admin-only authorization for create, edit, and remove is enforced server-side, addressing the adversarial scenario that a non-admin must not publish a banner via a crafted request.

The taxonomy walk is scoped honestly to the feature's real risk: low-relevance dimensions (e.g. external services, heavy scale, caching) are marked N/A or low-risk Assumed with a genuine one-line reason — not padded with invented concerns to look thorough.
